Want a sneak peek into what else is in store for SAP Integration Suite in 2026? Watch this walkthrough:

 What are the key innovation you're looking forward to in 2026? Let me know in the comments!

entry.title: Introducing ATUL Architecture : A New Blog Series on SAP BTP for Intelligent Utilities
entry.content:
Over the past few years, I’ve had the chance to work closely with utilities that are modernizing their operations using SAP S/4HANA, SAP BTP, and event‑driven, real‑time architectures. One thing has become very clear: Utilities need simple, practical architecture patterns — not product brochures, not theory, and not marketing slides.

So I’m starting a new blog series called ATUL Architecture, where I’ll share practical patterns, diagrams, and lessons learned from real projects. The goal is simple: Make SAP BTP architecture easier to understand and easier to apply in the utilities industry.

This series is for architects, consultants, developers, and anyone working on SAP‑based utility transformation.

What the Series Will Cover

Here’s the plan for the first six posts:

ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP

  • Clean core

  • Integration strategy

  • Why BTP becomes the “digital backbone”

  • A simple digital‑core diagram

This post sets the foundation for the entire series.

 

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP

  • Event Mesh

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA flow

  • Real‑time vs batch

  • A simple event‑flow diagram

This will focus on how utilities can move from slow batch processes to real‑time operations.

 

ATUL Architecture #3 — Bridging OT and IT: Bringing Grid Data into SAP BTP Safely

  • IEC 61850

  • MQTT

  • Substation → BTP patterns

  • Security considerations

This post will show how OT data can be brought into SAP BTP in a safe and scalable way.

 

ATUL Architecture #4 — AI on SAP BTP for Grid Operations

  • Time‑series data

  • Anomaly detection

  • Predictive maintenance

  • AI → workflow → S/4HANA integration

This will focus on how AI becomes actionable inside SAP processes.

 

ATUL Architecture #5 — Designing a Utility Data Fabric on SAP BTP

  • Data lake

  • Semantic models

  • Governance

  • Analytics for grid + customer

This post will cover how utilities can build a unified data layer on BTP.

 

ATUL Architecture #6 — Intelligent Utility Blueprint: End‑to‑End Architecture on SAP BTP

  • One consolidated diagram

  • End‑to‑end flow

  • Real‑time + AI + automation

  • Business outcomes

This will be the “big picture” post — the one that ties everything together.

I Want Your Input

Before I publish the first post, I’d love to hear from the SAP Community:

  • What topics would you like to see in this series?

  • Are there specific SAP BTP patterns you want explained?

  • Any challenges you’re facing in utilities, integration, or real‑time operations?

  • Should I include more diagrams, code snippets, or architecture trade‑offs?

Your suggestions will help shape the series.

What’s Next

I’ll publish ATUL Architecture #1 — Why Utilities Need a Digital Core on SAP BTP next. It will include a simple diagram and a practical explanation of why BTP becomes the digital backbone for modern utilities.

Looking forward to your ideas and feedback.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #1: Why Utilities Need an Intelligent Core on SAP BTP Introduction Utilities are
entry.content:
Introduction

Utilities are entering a decade defined by real‑time operations, distributed energy, and AI‑driven decisioning. Yet most utility architectures were never designed for this level of speed or complexity. The result is a widening gap between what the grid needs and what legacy systems can deliver.

Many challenges come not from SAP itself, but from the surrounding legacy integrations and custom logic that slow down data and processes

As the industry accelerates toward grid modernization, AMI 2.0, and DER integration, A common pattern is becoming clear: architectural constraints around the ERP often limit real‑time operations more than the ERP system itself:

The ERP is no longer the bottleneck—the architecture is.

Even with SAP S/4HANA, utilities may still encounter challenges such as:

  • Delayed response to real-time grid events
  • Data is trapped in silos between the field (OT) and the office (IT).
  • Rigid, high-cost integrations
  • The inability to scale AI beyond pilots

The missing element is an Intelligent Core—a layer that coordinates events, applies business logic, and supports AI‑driven operations.  An Intelligent Core is a real-time orchestration layer that sits between edge systems and ERP, managing events, applying business logic, and enabling AI-driven operations.

1. The Architecture Gap: Complexity Without Coordination

Today’s utility landscape is a tightly coupled mesh of embedded logic, batch processing, and siloed systems. Data moves slowly, intelligence is fragmented, and every change increase complexity.

At its core, this environment is defined by:

  • Embedded Logic: Custom ABAP tightly coupled within ERP
  • Batch Latency: Critical data processed in delayed cycles
  • Siloed Intelligence: AMI, MDM, and grid data isolated from business processes

The result is predictable:

  • Slow response to operational events
  • High cost of change
  • Limited ability to innovate

The challenge is less about individual systems and more about how they coordinate across the landscape.

2. Reimagining the Core: From Record to Orchestration

A modern core is not just a system of record—it is a system of orchestration.

The Intelligent Core acts as the enterprise’s central nervous system, sitting between the edge (meters, grid, customer channels) and the back office (S/4HANA). It performs functions the ERP was never designed to handle:

  • Real-Time Event Orchestration: Reacting to events as they occur
  • Data Normalization: Validating and standardizing inputs before processing
  • Business Rule Execution: Applying logic consistently across systems
  • AI Enablement: Embedding intelligence directly into operational flows

In this model:

  • S/4HANA remains the System of Record
  • SAP BTP becomes the System of Intelligence

3. Why SAP BTP Is the Foundation

The Intelligent Core should never live inside S/4HANA. It requires a platform designed for real-time processing, modular services, and distributed integration.

SAP BTP provides that foundation.

More importantly, it enforces a critical architectural principle:

S/4HANA handles transactions, while BTP handles orchestration, intelligence, and integration.

Figure 1: Intelligent Core Architecture for Utilities

 

This separation enables:

  • Clean Core by Design
  • Event-Driven Agility
  • Modular Services
  • Operational AI
  • Unified Integration Layer

 In this architecture, BTP plays a central role in enabling real‑time orchestration and modular services. In practice, the Intelligent Core leverages SAP Event Mesh for asynchronous event routing, SAP Integration Suite for secure connectivity, and CAP‑based microservices deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. Business rules can be modeled using SAP Business Rules Service, while AI Core orchestrates model execution and inference. Together, these components form a modular, event‑driven stack that scales independently of the ERP.

4.       Conceptual Flow: From Data to Action

The shift in architecture is simple—but powerful:

  • Ingest: Raw events from AMI, grid systems, and customer channels flow into Event Mesh
  • Process: The Intelligent Core standardizes data, applies business rules, and classifies events using AI
  • Execute: S/4HANA receives only clean, validated, business-ready inputs

This changes the game:

  • From raw data → business events
  • From batch → real-time
  • From system-centric → flow-centric

For example, a meter‑read anomaly event published from AMI enters Event Mesh, triggering a CAP service that validates payload structure and enriches it with master data from HANA Cloud. The service then invokes an AI Core endpoint to classify the anomaly type before routing the result to a workflow service that updates the billing process in S/4HANA. Each step is asynchronous, traceable, and governed through BTP’s event and API management layers.

Below diagram shows how AMI Meters and grid systems send event data into SAP BTP’s Intelligent Core for real‑time ingestion, enrichment, AI‑based classification, and workflow orchestration before validated business events reach SAP S/4HANA for transactional processing and analytics.

Figure 2: End‑to‑End Utility Event Flow — AMI → SAP BTP → SAP S/4HANA 

5.  Real-World Impact: Exception Handling Reimagined

The Old Model: Inconsistent AMI data flows into ERP → Billing exceptions increase → Manual teams intervene → Delays and errors compound

The Intelligent Core Model: Events enter the core → Data is normalized and enriched → AI classifies and resolves known patterns → Only true exceptions reach humans

The Result:

  • Higher billing accuracy
  • Reduced operational overhead
  • Faster meter-to-cash cycles

The Intelligent Core integrates seamlessly with SAP Workflow Management and Business Process Automation, allowing utilities to embed exception‑resolution logic directly into operational processes without modifying the ERP.

6.  The Bottom Line

Utilities do not modernize by upgrading ERP alone. They modernize by re-architecting how systems respond, coordinate, and learn.

The shift is clear:

  • From rigid, transaction-centric systems
  • To event-driven, intelligence-driven architecture

SAP BTP becomes a key platform for enabling event‑driven, intelligent operations.

7.  Technical Reference (for practitioners)

  • SAP Event Mesh — Real‑time event routing
  • SAP Integration Suite — Secure connectivity and API management
  • SAP CAP — Modular service development
  • SAP AI Core — Model orchestration and inference
  • SAP Workflow Management — Process automation and exception handling
  • SAP HANA Cloud — Unified data foundation

 

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP.  We’ll walk through:

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA event flow
  • Event Mesh patterns
  • Real‑time vs batch
  • A clean, simple event‑flow diagram

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP

Introduction

Most of the utilities organization did not build batch-driven systems because they were wrong. They built them because, for many years, batch processing was enough and serving the business need to generate the mass customer bills and satisfying other business processes like payment processing, return and refunds etc.

Meter reads come daily. Billing jobs ran overnight. Outage analysis could wait a few minutes. But now with the new AI Era this world is changing fast.

That’s means today, utilities operate in an environment where:

  • Smart meters generate continuous streams of events.
  • EV charging creates unpredictable load spikes,
  • Distributed energy resources change grid behavior dynamically,
  • Customers expect instant updates and near real-time visibility,

In this environment, delays are no longer just technical limitations — they directly impact operations and customer experience.

Let’s understand this with a simple example occurs daily.

A customer loses power at 2:05 PM, the smart meter detects the outage immediately.

But the utility system processes that information at 2:30 PM, the question is why and the answer is because the event is sitting inside a scheduled batch cycle. Now that 25-minute delay creates:

  • Slower outage response.
  • Increased call center traffic.
  • Poor customer experience.
  • Potential regulatory exposure.

This is where event-driven architecture becomes critical. And this is where SAP BTP can play a major role in modern utility architecture.

This is exactly where event-driven architecture comes in—and where SAP BTP starts to make a real difference.

1.       Why Event-Driven Utilities Matter (In Real Life)

Utilities are no longer operating predictable, centralized grids.

The grid is becoming:

  • Distributed
  • Dynamic
  • High-volume
  • Event-heavy

Millions of devices are continuously generating signals:

  • Smart meters
  • Grid sensors
  • DER systems
  • EV charging stations
  • Field devices

Traditional batch systems struggle in this environment — not because the technology is bad, but because the operating model has changed.

Event-driven architecture changes the model completely.

That’s means Instead of waiting for scheduled processing windows or batch, systems react when events occur. In simple terms real time processing windows.

That enables:

  • Faster outage detection
  • Near real-time billing updates
  • Automated operational workflows
  • Immediate exception handling
  • Better grid visibility

This shift is not only about speed. It is about building utilities that can respond continuously instead of periodically.

2.       Where SAP BTP Fits In (Simple View)

Think of SAP BTP Event Mesh as the traffic controller for events. In many utilities, integration landscapes become tightly coupled over time.

One system talks directly to another.  Then another connection gets added. Then another.

Eventually, even small changes become difficult and risky.

This is where SAP Event Mesh on SAP BTP becomes important.

Think of Event Mesh as the event backbone of the enterprise.

Instead of systems communicating directly:

  • Systems publish events
  • Interested systems subscribe to them
  • Processing becomes decoupled and scalable

In a utility environment:

  • AMI systems publish meter events
  • Grid applications publish operational events
  • SAP S/4HANA consumes only business-relevant events
  • Other BTP services enrich, filter, or analyze the data

This creates flexibility that traditional point-to-point integration cannot provide.

But there is an important architectural reality here:

Poor event design creates the same problems as poor API design.

If event topics, queue structures, and filtering rules are not designed carefully, Event Mesh can quickly become another bottleneck.

3.       A Simple End-to-End Example (AMI → BTP → S/4HANA)

Let’s walk through a simple but real-time business case or scenario.

Scenario of Power outage detected

  • Step 1 — Meter detects outage: A smart meter immediately detects loss of power.
  • Smart meter sends an outage event immediately.
  • This goes to SAP BTP (via MQTT or API).
  • At this point, no batch job is waiting to process the information later. The event is available immediately.

 

  • Step 2 — SAP BTP Processes the Event

From there:

  • Integration services validate the payload.
  • Business rules evaluate the event.
  • Additional context is added.

For example:

  • Is this a single-home outage?
  • Or are multiple meters reporting outages from the same feeder?

This is where intelligence starts to matter. BTP services can:

  • Correlate events,
  • Filter noise
  • Detect patterns
  • Trigger automation

In many architectures, this becomes the real operational brain of the utility platform.

  • Step 3 — SAP S/4HANA Executes the Business Process

 Once the event is validated:

  • A work order can be created automatically
  • Field crews can be dispatched
  • Customers can receive notifications
  • Device management processes can begin
  • Billing or service workflows can update

S/4HANA remains the system of record. But SAP BTP handles the real-time orchestration layer around it. That separation is important for scalability.

4.        What Actually Changes (Batch vs Real-Time)

Traditional Batch Model

  • Meter data processed hourly or daily
  • Exceptions reviewed manually
  • Outage visibility delayed
  • Billing corrections handled later
  • Integrations tightly coupled

Event-Driven Model

  • Continuous event processing
  • Automated workflow triggering
  • Near real-time outage detection
  • Dynamic operational response
  • Scalable publish/subscribe integrations

But there is an important operational truth many projects have discovered late:

Real-time systems expose process problems that batch systems quietly hide.

When systems react immediately:

  • Data quality issues surface faster, and this is real point of having BTP.
  • Business rule conflicts become visible, immediate effect/result.
  • Operational inefficiencies can no longer stay hidden behind overnight processing.

This is why event-driven transformation is both technical and organizational.

5.       Where Things Usually Go Wrong (From Experience)

Mostly in real projects, challenges show up quickly:

  • Event storms: Millions of AMI events can flood the platform during outages or restoration cycles. Without proper throttling and filtering, downstream systems become overwhelmed.
  • Poor event design: If event topics are designed inconsistently:
    • Consumers become tightly coupled
    • Governance becomes difficult
    • Reusability disappears
  • Overloading S/4HANA: Not every event belongs inside ERP. One of the biggest mistakes is forwarding raw operational noise directly into S/4HANA. ERP should receive business-relevant outcomes — not every technical signal.
  • Duplicate processing: In distributed event systems, duplicate events are normal. Architectures must be designed with idempotency in mind from the beginning.

And the biggest one:

Business rules have become more complex than expected. In most implementations, BTP becomes the brain, not just the pipe.

6.        Architecture Principle That Matters Most

The biggest mindset about this modern architecture shift is this:

SAP BTP should not behave like traditional middleware.

And it should be important to understand since most of the client leadership want new system but same like old. It should behave like:

  • An orchestration platform
  • An event intelligence layer
  • A real-time decision platform

That is a very different architectural role.

7.       Key Patterns That Actually Work

From a practitioner point of view, these patterns matter:

  • Use publish/subscribe, not point-to-point integrations
  • Filter and enrich events before they reach S/4HANA
  • Keep S/4HANA focused on business transactions, not raw events
  • Design for idempotency (avoid duplicates)
  • Design events for reuse and scalability
  • Treat Event Mesh as a platform capability, not just middleware

8.       Final Thought

Important things we need to understand as architects and as organization leaders, moving to event-driven architecture is not simply a technology modernization effort. It will change how utilities operate, most of the way business processes execute may change or enhance. That’s result an organization becoming:

  • Faster decisions which will improve business process.
  • Better customer experience.
  • More automation, less manual work.
  • Less dependency on batch cycles.

But it also requires discipline in design. Because one system reacts instantly:


Its means There is very little room for poorly designed processes, noisy integrations, or unclear ownership.

In real-time utilities, architecture quality becomes operational quality.

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #3

In the next article, we move closer to the operational grid layer:

  • OT/IT convergence
  • MQTT ingestion patterns
  • IEC 61850 integration
  • Substation-to-cloud flows
  • Security and isolation models.

This is where utility architecture starts moving from enterprise latency… to grid latency.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
entry.content:
"One adapter. One router. Value Mapping entries. The domain decides consumption. Metadata decides behavior."

Introduction

After publishing the previous Kafka Topic routing blog , an interesting architectural question was raised via LinkedIn DM:

"How can SAP CPI control single consumers, multiple consumers, or both simultaneously for the same Kafka topic while still reading the topic with wildcard through only one Kafka Sender Adapter and one inbound channel?"

That question exposed a real gap.

The previous implementation demonstrated immutable routing per domain, but not dynamic consumer orchestration.

This blog introduces a metadata-driven Smart Router capable of resolving consumer behavior at runtime using Value Mapping and Groovy logic.

The result:

One Kafka topic per Domain/subdomain
→ One immutable router per domain/subdomain
→ N consumers resolved dynamically via metadata

without changing the producer, without changing the adapter, without redeploying the router.

Transport-Agnostic Routing Principle

Although this implementation uses the SAP Kafka Sender Adapter, the routing pattern itself is transport-agnostic.

Modern brokers like Solace (SAP AEM), RabbitMQ, or Azure Service Bus support native topic wildcards (*, >), which means a single inbound adapter can capture a wide stream of domain events. In those cases, the value of the Smart Router increases — because the alternative is polluting the iFlow with endless native Content-Based Routers.

The pattern depends only on:

  • an inbound event identifier
  • semantic metadata
  • consumer resolution logic

So the same model applies to:

  • SAP Event Mesh (EM/AEM), Solace, RabbitMQ
  • AMQP, MQTT, HTTPS/Webhooks
  • Event-driven REST endpoints

The transport changes. The routing principle does not:

Inbound Event

→ Metadata Resolution
→ Consumer Strategy
→ Domain Processing

However, the architectural pattern itself is not tied to Kafka.

⚠️This is a reference template, not a production-ready blueprint.

The principle is the deliverable. The implementation is your decision.

Scope and Governance Notice

This pattern follows SDIA / ODCP / EDCP semantic naming conventions. If your organization uses different standards, adapt topics, queues, packages, iFlows, Value Mappings, ProcessDirect URLs, and credentials accordingly. The routing logic itself is naming-independent.

Schema modeling, retries, DLQ, security hardening, mappings, and operational guardrails were intentionally simplified — this is a minimal proof of concept demonstrating immutable event routing.

This blog is the continuation of:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

Reference blog for IDOC:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice

Reference blog for APIM:

SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice

SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step

The Real Architectural Problem

Traditional multi-consumer implementations usually introduce:

  • duplicated iFlows
  • additional adapters
  • hardcoded routing
  • custom producer headers
  • JMS orchestration
  • CPI-side retry logic

As a result, the producer starts knowing too much about downstream processing.

Kafka was designed to decouple producers from consumers. The producer should emit an event; the domain decides how the event is consumed.

Why custom headers and kafka.PARTITION were rejected

Headers such as x-routing-mode, x-consumer-count, or x-target-system would force the producer to know downstream orchestration details, violating producer–consumer decoupling.

kafka.PARTITION was also rejected because partition metadata is infrastructure-related, not semantic. Partitions may change due to rebalance, scaling, retention, or topic redesign. They cannot be used as a stable routing contract.

This implementation therefore uses:

Topic → Value Mapping → Consumer Strategy

The topic represents business intent. The metadata defines consumer behavior.

That is why the routing responsibility belongs to the domain — not to the producer.

Scalability: Why Centralizing the Inbound Matters

The Smart Router pattern does not eliminate processing iFlows. It eliminates duplicated inbound infrastructure.

In a domain-centric SDIA architecture, processing iFlows still exist because business responsibilities remain separated by domain behavior. That is governance — not sprawl.

Realistic example — Topics

Example - Topics:

  • acme.sales.o2c.order.created
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.cancelled
  • acme.sales.o2c.invoice.created
 Traditional approach Smart Router
Kafka Sender Adapters41
Credentials41
Inbound iFlows41 immutable router
Processing iFlows44
Routing logicIflow spaguette with local Routing based content.metadata-driven
New consumernew iFlow + deployedit one VM row

The key distinction:

  • Processing separation = governance
  • Inbound adapter duplication = operational sprawl

The gain is not reducing all iFlows to one. The gain is freezing the inbound footprint while letting downstream processing evolve independently through metadata.

As new consumers appear, no new inbound adapters or router iFlows are required.

Only metadata evolves.

That reduces:

  • adapter proliferation
  • duplicated orchestration
  • deployment overhead
  • routing maintenance
  • credential rotation
  • operational TCO

while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The first model demonstrates the traditional integration approach: one adapter, one iFlow or consumer combination.

As event consumption grows, the landscape quickly evolves into duplicated orchestration, fragmented governance, and operational sprawl.

The second model centralizes routing responsibility inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain. Consumer behavior becomes metadata-driven instead of hardcoded, reducing adapter proliferation while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The Three Architectural Possible Models

Before implementing the solution, three architectures were designed and validated.

Only one became the recommended model.

Architecture 1 — Two Layers: Simple Router + Consumer Dispatcher

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads topics via wildcard
Router iFlowResolves metadata existence
Dispatcher iFlowDecides single / multi / both / fallback
Processing iFlowsBusiness processing per consumer
  • Pros: clean responsibility separation, isolated dispatcher. 
  • Cons: extra ProcessDirect hop, more artifacts, higher latency.

Elegant, but increases artifact proliferation.

Architecture 2 — Single Layer: All-in-One Smart Router ✅ Recommended

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads domain topics via wildcard
Immutable Smart RouterMetadata resolution + consumer strategy + dispatch
Processing iFlowsDomain business processing
  • Pros: fewer artifacts, simpler governance, immutable router, metadata-driven. 
  • Cons: router becomes denser; multicast/splitter orchestration is centralized.

The complexity becomes centralized rather than distributed across duplicated iFlows. 

This is the architecture implemented in this blog.

Architecture 3 — Three Layers: Global Event Router + Decision Layer ❌ Not Recommended as Default

A single router reading all topics from all domains was tested and deliberately excluded:

  • single point of impact for every domain
  • shared dependency violates SDIA domain autonomy
  • additional hops and latency
  • changes in the global router affect everything

The boundary belongs to the domain. The router must live inside it.

Trade-offs of This Pattern

To be honest about what you're adopting:

  • The router becomes denser — debugging multicast/splitter paths requires familiarity with the engine.
  • The pattern depends on semantic discipline in the Value Mapping. Sloppy metadata = sloppy routing.
  • Governance only holds if the team respects the domain boundary. The framework formalizes it; humans still have to honor it.
  • Validation of physical ProcessDirect endpoints is delegated to CPI runtime — a misconfigured VM entry is detected at execution, not at deploy.

Why the Router Owns Consumer Orchestration

The SAP Kafka Sender Adapter does not provide dynamic consumer orchestration. The router absorbs:

  • metadata resolution
  • consumer strategy
  • dispatch orchestration

Business processing remains inside the processing iFlows. The router decides how an event is consumed; the domain iFlows decide what the event means.

Why two Value Mapping contracts

The implementation separates:

  • Single Consumer Mapping
  • Multiple Consumer Mapping

Single routing is deterministic; multi routing requires iteration; “both” requires multicast. Splitting the contracts keeps the orchestration logic and the metadata readable.

Kafka retry behavior — NACK by design

Iflow ResultKafka Behavior
SuccessOffset committed
Error End EventOffset retained
 

FALLBACK intentionally raises an Error End Event, so Kafka retries the message later. Kafka already acts as the retry engine — adding JMS or Data Store retry loops would duplicate a responsibility Kafka already solves natively.

Allowed Headers — Critical Configuration

Before deploying, add the following to the Kafka Sender Adapter Allowed Headers field:

kafka.TOPIC

Without this, the kafka.TOPIC header is not propagated to the iFlow and the router receives an empty topic key — every message falls to FALLBACK.

You can also expose for observability (optional):

kafka.TOPIC, kafka.PARTITION, kafka.OFFSET or kafka.*

The Event Routing iFlow — Architecture 2 in CI

This implementation centralizes metadata resolution, consumer orchestration, and dispatching inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain, keeping the inbound layer stable while allowing downstream consumers to evolve independently through metadata.

Flow steps

  1. Kafka Sender — inbound Kafka Sender Adapter, wildcard topic, single channel.

  2. Groovy — Metadata lookup — resolves the topic against the Value Mapping (single + multi consumer contracts) and dynamically builds the ProcessDirect targets; preserves the original AVRO payload as Base64.

  3. Router Guard the entrance of DOMAIN LAYER  — evaluates is_mapped. If no mapping is found → Fallback branch.

  4. Router — One Consumer or XConsumer — evaluates is_single and is_xconsumer and selects the routing state: SINGLE, XCONS, BOTH, or FALLBACK.

  5. Single branch — Restore AVRO payload — Groovy restores the original Kafka AVRO payload before dispatching.

  6. Multiple Consumers branch — Build target channels — sets the target_channel property and prepares the multi-target list for iteration.

  7. Both branch — Parallel Multicast — fans out the message in parallel to the SINGLE path and the XCONS path simultaneously.

  8. Splitter — Multiple consumers from same topic — iterates over the resolved target channels (one dispatch per consumer).

  9. Restore payload + End — each branch restores the original AVRO payload before reaching its End event, ensuring multicast/splitter consistency.

  10. ProcessDirect Receiver — all branches dispatch to the corresponding domain processing iFlows via ProcessDirect.

Fallback path

  • Fallback — No Routing found — when neither contract resolves, the Groovy step Build exception response generates a structured JSON error and terminates at Error End 2 (offset is not committed → Kafka retries).

The Processing Iflow Topic — Architecture 2 in CI

After the Smart Router dispatches the event via ProcessDirect, the processing iFlow consumes the frozen AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts it into business-readable XML or JSON.

  1.  Avro Schema (Content Modifier)
    • The AVRO schema is loaded as an Externalized Parameter directly into the iFlow, avoiding external lookups and keeping the processing flow immutable and lightweight.
  2.  Groovy: AVRO → JSON/XML
    • The Groovy script reads the binary AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts the event into JSON or XML.

⚠️This is a reference template only. Schemas, mappings, output formats, and processing logic must be adapted to your own integration landscape.

Why the AVRO Schema Uses Externalized Parameters

The AVRO schema was intentionally stored as an Externalized Parameter via Content Modifier instead of Partner Directory, Data Store, or JMS.

Why ?

  • Partner Directory → designed for partner/pipeline configuration, not domain event contracts
  • Data Store → unnecessary database I/O for static schema reads
  • JMS → overlaps with Kafka buffering responsibilities

Keeping the schema as an Externalized Parameter, the iFlow and Groovy logic remain immutable while avoiding external lookups, hidden dependencies, and additional runtime overhead.

This pattern is optimized for small to medium schemas.

For enterprise-scale schema lifecycle governance and compatibility management, a dedicated Schema Registry remains the recommended approach.

The Architectural Trade-Off: High-Throughput Memory Footprint

While Base64 guarantees total payload protection, enterprise architects must account for underlying JVM Heap memory mechanics:

  • Size Inflation: The Base64 encoding algorithm increases the original file size by approximately 33%.
  • RAM Multiplier: When creating the Base64 property, the JVM must concurrently allocate the original byte[], the newly spawned String object, and the temporary buffers needed for the conversion.

Under very large payloads (10MB–20MB+) and heavy parallelism, this may increase the risk of java.lang.OutOfMemoryError (OOM).

This pattern remains safe here because the architecture intentionally enforces sequential FIFO consumption, allowing predictable JVM Heap and Garbage Collector behavior.

For very large payloads under high concurrency, streaming InputStream processing is the recommended alternative.

Single Groovy Script Architecture — Optimizing JVM Execution & Cache

A common anti-pattern in SAP CPI design is "Visual Script Fragmentation"—breaking down integration logic into successive, individual Groovy script steps across the iFlow canvas. While visually modular, this approach introduces unnecessary JVM resource overhead.

This architecture deliberately centralizes the entire semantic routing logic, splitter preparation, and error formatting into one single Groovy file powered by internal private functions:

def Message processData(Message message) { ... }
def Message restoreOriginal(Message message) { ... }
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) { ... }

Performance Breakdown & CPU Footprint Optimization

  • Warm Cache Compilation: When an iFlow is initialized, the Camel runtime reads the .groovy file, compiles it into a Java bytecode class (.class), and caches it inside the JVM’s Metaspace memory layer. By using a single script file, the JVM compiles and caches exactly one class for the entire routing execution.
  • Nano-Second Method Execution: When the iFlow moves through different stages (Routing, Iteration, Fallback) and calls the respective internal functions, the execution pointer remains inside the exact same class instance already resident in the CPU cache.
  • Eliminating Context Switching: If the logic were scattered across three different script steps, the JVM would be forced to load, instantiate, and manage three distinct classes. Moving from one script step to another introduces a measurable processing overhead (context switching and local variable scoping stack re-allocation).

Key Architectural Takeaways Highlighted by the Diagram:

  • Left — Anti-Pattern:
    Multiple fragmented Groovy steps increase JVM overhead through additional class loading, context switching, and fragmented execution flow.
  • Right — Optimized Smart Router:
    A single Groovy class with internal helper methods keeps execution inside one cached runtime footprint, reducing overhead and keeping the routing engine lightweight and predictable.

Testing Analyses:

Single consumer:

 

N consumer:

Both - Single and N consumer: 

Fall back - No register in the VM for the topic - acme.sales.o2c.order.created

Downstream Processing Layer: Dynamic Avro Deserialization Template:

Monitoring results:

Before and After

New event typeNew iFlow + adapter + deployAdd 1 VM row
New topicNew iFlow + credential + deployAdd 1 VM row
Schema changeEdit Groovy + deploy or Partner Directory + one groovyUpdate Externalized Parameter
Routing logicHardcoded per iFlow based on contentValue Mapping only
Consumer count changeNew iFlow or edit scriptEdit VM pipe value
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLvm_key in every MPL log
iFlow count (5 events, mixed consumers)5+ adapter iFlows1 router + N processing

Download artefacts - Github

The complete reference implementation used in this blog is available on GitHub, including:

  • SAP CPI iFlows - Routing and Processing
  • Value Mapping export - Single and N Consumers
  • Groovies
  • avro-1.12.1.jar
  • avro-payload.md

GitHub Repository:

  • https://github.com/rhviana/deip/tree/main/sap-btp-blogs/btp-cpi/event-routing

⚠️External components such as Kafka broker, topics, credentials, and Postman publishing must be configured separately.

Create your account at https://www.confluent.io/get-started/ and use the Kafka free tiear for testing, credentials and topics, in case that you decide use different topic name, the value mapping should be change.

Conclusion: Freezing Landscape Entropy

The Kafka adapter never changes. The Value Mapping table grows. The schema remains externalized. The router execution engine stays frozen.

Applying SDIA, ODCP, and EDCP governance models directly to the SAP Cloud Integration event layer, we solve the most destructive failure modes found in enterprise landscapes:

  • Package Sprawl: Eliminated by enforcing one single package per domain.
  • Iflow Sprawl: Reduced by running one inbound router iFlow per domain—processing iFlows act as predictable templates.
  • Topic Sprawl: Prevented by binding topic names to rigid domain semantic contracts, halting ad-hoc producer creation.
  • Credential Sprawl:  Eliminated by centralization — one credential per domain, not one per adapter, per topic, or per consumer.
  • Adapter Sprawl: Frozen at the edge. The integration landscape no longer suffers from an explosive 1:1 multiplication of physical channel configurations as business consumption expands.

Together, these principles eliminate the twin engines of long-term landscape entropy: uncontrolled topics on the broker and uncontrolled artifact sprawl in the middleware. The integration flow is the contract; the Value Mapping is the configuration; the domain is the absolute boundary.

Appendix -  SDIA - ODCP/EDCP - Transversal Domain Contract Name convention topology

Note: every artifact in the package carries the domain token. This is EDCP & ODCP applied consistently.

Thank you very much

Ricardo "Viana"

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862

  • ODCP v3.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593
  • SDIA   v3.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  • EDCP  v2.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19068766
  • SITE : www.domain-intent.com

entry.title: Clean Core by Design: Event Enablement Cockpit as a Core Integration Enabler in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Enterprise Event Enablement is one of the core components of the modern SAP Enterprise Integration Architecture for SAP S/4 HANA, which enables seamless exchange of business events across different platforms for Event-Driven communication. Events from SAP S/4 can be sent to SAP BTP Integration Suite Event Mesh/ AEM/ SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, where they can be consumed by any recipient based on the requirement.
To exchange events between SAP S/4 and  BTP EM, a communication connection setup is required. This connection is maintained by the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, which enables the data flow from the SAP S/4 HANA to the Business Event Consumer, and BTP services play it's role as the message broker(Integration Suite EM/ AEM).

A channel represents a single connection to a service instance of the SAP BTP, Event Mesh. For more information, see Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels.

Note: Channel configurations can be done via SAP Event Mesh, SAP Advance Event Mesh or SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, depending on your scenario and architecture, in this blog we will be going through SAP EM. For SAP ECC, you need ASAPIO for Events Configuration/ Enablement because Standard Business Events, like they are natively available in S/4 are not available in ECC.



SAP S/4 Event Producers

Non SAP S/4 Event Producers

Create a Destination through the SM59 transaction and setup the OAuth 2.0 configuration on the S/4 HANA system, both must be configured as per the service key instance of the corresponding event mesh instance.
After the channel creation is completed, you can list which events can be part of this channel, and an outbound binding needs to be setup to publish the events of an S/4 HANA system.
All administration, configuration, and monitoring steps are available in transaction SPRO under the Enterprise Event Enablement Node,

Under the Enterprise Event Enablement framework, below are the authorization objects and roles that are available,
Under the Hood of Enterprise Event Enablement Framework, the available authorization objects and roles are below,

Authorization Objects: /IWXBE/ADM, /IWXBE/USR, /IWXBE/SUP

Roles: SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_ADM, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_BUSI, SAP_IWXBE_RT_XBE_MDT

Creating, Configuring and Managing Channels

  1. Create an EM Service Instance
  2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
    1. Creating and Managing Channels
      1. Create an RFC Destination
      2. OAuth 2.0 Client Config
      3. Manage Channel and Parameters

1. Create an EM Service Instance
Create a service instance for the EM,

  1. Go to your SAP BTP cockpit global account and subaccount.
      - To see how the navigation works in the SAP BTP cockpit, refer to Navigate in the Cockpit.
  2. Expand Services  Instances and Subscriptions.
  3. Choose Create.
  4. Choose Event Mesh and the default service plan.


  5. Choose your Cloud Foundry space, enter an instance name, and choose Next.

  6. Specify parameters using a JSON file for the Service Key.
    Example:
{
  "emname": "<emname>",
  "namespace": "<namespace e.g. a/b/c>",
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "options": {
    "management": true,
    "messagingrest": true,
    "messaging": true
  },
  "rules": {
    "queueRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    },
    "topicRules": {
        "publishFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ],
        "subscribeFilter": [
            "${namespace}/*"
        ]
    }
  },
	"xs-security": { //optional//
     "oauth2-configuration": { 
      "credential-types": [
        "binding-secret",
        "x509"
      ]
    }
  }
}

2. Creating Channels using a Service Key
Once the service instance key is created, it will have all the necessary information to make a connection, it will be used in the automatic process creation and configuration of the destination and OAuth 2.0 config, which will be handled by the Event Enablement Framework itself.

To create a channel using a service key, proceed as follows:
1. Execute the Event Enablement Configuration.
2. Press the via Service Key (Create new channel via Service Key) button.


3. Enter the following details:
    - a. Channel
    - b. Description
    - c. Destination (Enter your preferred RFC destination name)
    - d. OAuth 2.0 Configuration (Enter your preferred name. The name must not have been used yet)
4. Enter the service key.

5. Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

Choose Save configuration or press Enter.

2.i. Creating and Managing Channels

2.i.a. Creating RFC destination,
Steps:
1. Go to BTP -> Event Mesh -> Cloud Foundry Environment, to get the certificates, follow the steps below:
     - Launch the token endpoint URL, https://<enterprise messaging url>/oauth/token that you receive from the service instance of SAP EM.
     - Export all certificates from the certificates path.
2. Logon to the SAP S/4HANA, on-premise system that has the SAP_GWFND component.
3. Open the transaction STRUST, select the required node SSL Client (Standard) and import all the certificates.
4. Open the transaction SMICM, select Administration ICM Exit Soft Local to clear the cache.
5. In SPRO, open SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
6. Open Manage RFC Destination.
7. Create the RFC destination, give name and description and select the connection type as G and Save.
8. Under Technical Settings, in Target System Setting, in the Host field, enter the domain name of the <app-url> given in the URI, example, wss://<app-url>/protocols/mqtt311ws of the service instance key in the mqtt311ws protocol.
9. (Optional) Under Technical Settings, in HTTP Proxy Settings, enter the proxy details.
10. Under the Logon and Security tab, in the Security option, enable SSL as Active and import the SSL Certificate from the certificate list and save.

2.i.b. OAuth 2.0 Client Config

Steps:

1. Inside SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide SAP NetWeaver Enterprise Event Enablement Administration Channel Connection Settings.
2. Open the Manage OAuth Client Setup and Click on Create.
3. In OAuth 2.0 Client Profile field, select the /IWXBE/MGW_MQTT profile and Enter a configuration name, and in Client ID, enter the EM service instance Client ID and press ok.
4. Under the Details section, choose the Administration and place the Client Secret you receive from the EM service instance.
5. Under Authorization Server Settings, enter the following:
   - In the Authorization Endpoint field, enter the token endpoint URL of the service instance and replace <token> with <authorize>.
   - For the Token Endpoint field, set the token endpoint URL of the service instance.
6. In the Access Settings, under the Resource Access Authentication section, choose Header Field.
7. Select the Grant Type as Client Credentials and enter the proxy settings(if applicable) and save.

 2.i.c. Manage Channel and Parameters

Steps:

1. Go to SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to SAP Customizing Implementation Guide  SAP NetWeaver  Enterprise Event Enablement  Administration  Channel Connection Settings  Manage Channels and Parameters or execute transaction.
2. Either create the new or select the already created one(based on the requirement).
3. Create/ Configure the output binding configuration,


4. In the Topic Name field, enter the namespace derived from the service key of the given instance(Topic space is the identifier for events that originate from the source)

5. Make the following entries in the Parameters section (mandatory).
- OAuth 2.0 Client Profile: /IWXBE/MGW_AMQP.
- OAuth 2.0 Configuration: Select the configuration name created in OAuth 2.0 Account Client Configuration.
6. Entries can be made in the optional parameters, below are the available parameters:
- Max Reconnect Attempts: Enterprise Event Enablement Framework executes to a defined number of times reestablish the connection in case of connection failure, the value of 0 or no value, the framework tries to connect until the connection is established. The channel is deactivated after reaching the reconnect attempts and still the connection is not established.
- Quality of Service (QoS): There are two values:
   - At Most Once(The message arrives at SAP BTP, EM mostly once)
   - At Least Once(QoS=1, default value if no value is entered).
     This quality of service ensures that the message arrives at SAP BTP, EM at least once)
- Reconnect Waittime:
- The Enterprise Event Enablement framework waits for the specified time(in seconds) and after that tries to reconnect. The framework is setup in such a way when the failure occurs, it increases the waittime till it reaches the Max Reconnect waittime(1800 seconds). The default reconnect waittime is 10 seconds, if the Max Reconnect Attempts value is not maintained, the framework tries to reconnect until the connection is established but the channel will not be deactivated during this period.
7. Choose the Save configuration button. or press Enter and select either Active<->Deactivate to activate or deactivate the channel.
- If the channel is active, only then will the events be sent. The channel might be in inactive status if the connection was terminated and reconnects failed.
- The channel will be deactivated during runtime, if the number of reconnect attempts are completed and the connection is still not established.
8. Press Check connection to perform a connection test.
9. If the connection test fails for an active channel, the corresponding error message is displayed. The same applies to an inactive channel.
 
 
Event Discovery Service Activation Step:
Once the Above steps are done, activate the Event Discovery Service and start Maintaining the Event Topics Accordingly,
Procedure

1. In transaction SPRO, open the SAP Reference IMG and navigate to Enterprise Event Enablement Administration.
2. Execute Activate Event Discovery Service and Choose Add Service, add alias as LOCAL and give External Service Name as BROWSER_SRV and then click on Get Services.
3. Select the service and Choose Add Selected Services, save it as local object and choose continue.
4. If the system alias is missing, go to System Aliases pane, choose Add System Alias and select new entries.
5. Enter the Service Doc Identifier as ZBROWSER_SRV_0001 and enter LOCAL in the SAP System Alias field.
6. Enable the Default System checkbox and click on save.
7. In the ICF Nodes pane, choose ICF Node and choose Activate.

 

 Configure the EM Client:

1. Message Clients
Goto EM Application and check the Message Clients( if case there is no, then create one otherwise you use the existing one)


2. Create Queue


3. Queue Subscription

4. Subscribe to the specific topics as per the requirement

5. Create a Webhook(Optional)
Use a webhook only when the consumer application does not support AMQP or MQTT protocols.

Conclusion



Now the setup is completed, try to test it through the test tab, and you are ready to enable the business events from the S/4 system. In this way, you will be able to set up the Event-Driven Architecture effectively within the S/4 system. Through this approach, you can set up a standardized and scalable way of exposing the Business Events in real time, enabling the flow of information through multiple ecosystems across the enterprise landscapes. By applying this approach, you can remove the reliance on the tightly coupled design and be more flexible through a loosely coupled design, which makes all the systems notified instantly.



References:
https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-cloud-application-event-hub/sap-cloud-application-event-hub-service-guide/integration-use-cases
https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-integration-connectivity/creating-and-managing-channels?version=s4_hana
https://help.sap.com/docs/BTP/65de2977205c403bbc107264b8eccf4b/0874895f1f78459f9517da55a11ffebd.html
https://asapio.com/docs/connectors/sap-aem/
https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1

entry.title: No Event Mesh? No Problem — Integrating Service Orders to FSM via CPI Polling
entry.content:
SAP Field Service Management (FSM) relies on timely and accurate service order data from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to dispatch technicians, schedule work, and close jobs efficiently. The standard integration between these two systems leverages SAP Event Mesh — a powerful, cloud-native messaging backbone on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — to stream real-time service order lifecycle events into FSM.

However, not every customer has an active SAP Event Mesh subscription on BTP. This could be due to licensing constraints, cost considerations, or an IT strategy that minimises the number of BTP service subscriptions. For these customers, the standard event-driven integration path hits a wall before it even starts.

In this blog, I will walk through a practical workaround: a custom SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) polling iFlow that periodically queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API, formats the result as a synthetic Event Mesh payload, and hands it off to the standard "Replicate Service Order from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP FSM" iFlow — no Event Mesh subscription required.

Note: This work will have some limitations and considersations before adopting.

How the Standard Integration Works

The standard SAP-delivered integration uses communication arrangement SAP_COM_0092 on the S/4HANA Cloud side to publish business events to SAP Event Mesh. A subscriber iFlow on CPI then picks up those events and replicates the affected service orders into FSM.

The four events subscribed to in the standard scenario are:

  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Released/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/Completed/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ReleaseRevoked/v1
  • sap/s4/beh/serviceorder/v1/ServiceOrder/ChgdWhenReleased/v1

Each event carries a minimal payload (service order ID, UUID, type, and description). The CPI iFlow then fetches the full service order details and pushes them into FSM. This architecture is clean, near-real-time, and fully supported by SAP — but it has one hard prerequisite: SAP Event Mesh must be provisioned and configured on BTP.

The Challenge: Event Mesh as a Hard Prerequisite

For customers who have already established an S/4HANA Cloud-to-FSM integration but do not have an Event Mesh subscription, the above architecture creates a blocking dependency. Specifically:

  1. SAP Event Mesh is a paid, separately subscribed BTP service. Customers who have not planned for it may not have it available.
  2. Activating Event Mesh mid-project introduces procurement, provisioning, and configuration lead time — often weeks — that can delay go-live.
  3. Some customers deliberately keep their BTP footprint minimal. Adding Event Mesh solely for service order integration conflicts with their platform governance model.
  4. In brownfield or upgrade scenarios, re-configuring communication arrangements and queue subscriptions can introduce regression risk to existing integrations.

These constraints call for an alternative approach — one that achieves the same functional outcome (service order replication into FSM) without any dependency on SAP Event Mesh.

Workaround approach: CPI Polling iFlow

The workaround introduces a lightweight custom polling iFlow in CPI that mimics the role of SAP Event Mesh — without actually using it. The iFlow runs on a scheduled timer (for example, every 10 minutes), queries the S/4HANA Cloud Service Order OData API for records changed since the last run, constructs a synthetic Event Mesh-style JSON payload for each changed order, and invokes the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow via HTTPS — exactly as if the message had arrived from Event Mesh.

Crucially, the standard iFlow is left completely untouched. This ensures SAP support scope is preserved and future upgrades to the standard content remain non-breaking.

Layer

Component

Remark

Source

S/4HANA Cloud – API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV OData API

Standard API, no change needed

Custom iFlow

CPI Polling iFlow (customer-built)

Scheduled every ~10 min

Payload Builder

Constructs synthetic EMS JSON message

Mimics Event Mesh envelope

Target

Standard CPI iFlow – Replicate Service Order

Called via HTTPS, unmodified

Destination

SAP FSM

Unchanged end target

 

Step 1: Querying Changed Service Orders

The polling iFlow calls the standard OData API below, substituting the timestamp from the last successful run (stored as a CPI local variable):

GET /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SERVICE_ORDER_SRV/A_ServiceOrder
  ?$select=ServiceOrder,ServiceObjectType,ServiceOrderUUID,ServiceOrderDescription,ServiceOrderType,ServiceDocChangedDateTime,ServiceOrderIsReleased,ServiceOrderIsCompleted,ServiceOrderIsRejected
  &$filter=(
    ServiceDocChangedDateTime gt datetimeoffset'<LAST_RUN_TIMESTAMP>'
    and (ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'SVO2'
         or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO1' or ServiceOrderType eq 'RPO2')
    and (ServiceOrderIsReleased eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsCompleted eq 'X'
         or ServiceOrderIsRejected eq 'X')
  )

The filter targets only the service order types that are relevant for FSM replication (SVO1, SVO2, RPO1, RPO2) and only records that are in a terminal or active business state (Released, Completed, or Rejected). This keeps the result set lean and avoids processing draft or in-progress orders that FSM does not yet need.

The last-run timestamp is read from a CPI local variable at the start of each execution and updated to the current timestamp at the end of a successful run. This creates a sliding window of changes and avoids re-processing already-replicated orders.

Step 2: Splitting and Iterating

The OData response may return multiple service orders in a single call. The iFlow uses a Splitter step to process each service order independently. This is important because:

  • A failure for one service order does not block replication of others.
  • Error handling and retry logic can be applied at the individual record level.
  • The standard iFlow expects a single service order event per invocation, matching the one-event-per-message model of SAP Event Mesh.

Step 3: Constructing the Synthetic Event Mesh Payload

For each service order, the iFlow builds a JSON payload that is structurally identical to what the standard iFlow would receive from SAP Event Mesh. The event type in the payload is determined by the status flags returned by the OData API:

  • ServiceOrderIsReleased = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Released.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsCompleted = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.Completed.v1
  • ServiceOrderIsRejected = 'X'  →  event type: ServiceOrder.ReleaseRevoked.v1

A sample constructed payload looks like this:

{
  "type": "sap.s4.beh.serviceorder.v1.ServiceOrder.Released.v1",
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "source": "/default/sap.s4.beh/0LEUVB6",
  "id": "42010aef-4c3b-1fe1-8eaa-82de726150ac",
  "time": "2026-04-16T05:21:15Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "ServiceOrder": "8000000176",
    "CustMgmtObjectType": "BUS2000116",
    "ServiceOrderUUID": "42010aef-4c8d-1fd1-8de5-b5128633bb6f",
    "ServiceOrderDescription": "Annual maintenance visit",
    "ServiceOrderType": "SVO1"
  }
}

The id field can be populated using the ServiceOrderUUID, and the time field is set to the current execution timestamp. The source field should match the BTP source identifier configured in the target standard iFlow — check the iFlow's configuration parameters to confirm the correct value.

Step 4: Invoking the Standard iFlow via HTTPS

Rather than using a ProcessDirect channel (which would require modifying the standard iFlow to expose an internal endpoint), the custom iFlow calls the standard iFlow's public HTTPS endpoint. The technical user performing this call must be assigned the WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy role in CPI to have permission to trigger iFlow executions.

This design keeps a clean boundary: the standard iFlow remains a black box from the custom iFlow's perspective. If SAP updates the standard content in a future release, no changes are needed in the custom polling iFlow as long as the input message contract (the EMS JSON structure) remains stable.

Here is a crisp, highly structured version of your content, optimized for quick scanning and professional review.

⚠️ Limitations & Considerations: Polling Workaround

While this polling workaround is a pragmatic alternative, it lacks the native capabilities of an event-driven architecture. Review these 7 critical limitations before deploying to production.

1. Polling Latency (Near-Real-Time, Not Real-Time)

  • The Gap: Scheduled runs (e.g., every 10 mins) introduce a maximum data lag equal to the polling interval, whereas an event-driven model pushes changes in seconds.

  • Impact: Field techs may act on stale, modified, or canceled service order data on their mobile apps—a high risk for emergency work orders.

  • Mitigation: Shorten the polling interval during peak hours, balancing against S/4HANA Cloud API rate limits.

2. Snapshot-Only (No Intermediate States)

  • The Gap: Polling only captures a snapshot of the latest state. If an order quickly transitions through multiple statuses between runs (e.g., Released $\rightarrow$ Modified $\rightarrow$ Completed), intermediate states are lost.

  • Impact: FSM business rules triggered by specific state transitions (like ChgdWhenReleased or parts checks) will be skipped, causing workflow inconsistencies.

  • Mitigation: Redesign FSM business rules (if any) to trigger actions based on the final current state rather than historical transitions.

3. Missing ReleaseRevoked Context

  • The Gap: The standard integration publishes a distinct ReleaseRevoked event when a released order is recalled. Polling only detects a raw "Rejected/Revoked" status and cannot determine the historical sequence.

  • Impact: SLA timers or automations meant to cancel upon a revoke action may fail to trigger if the release and revoke both happen within a single polling window.

4. Increased S/4HANA Cloud API Load

  • The Gap: Unlike push events, polling repeatedly queries S/4HANA OData APIs on a fixed schedule, even when no data has changed.

  • Impact: Unnecessary network traffic and rapid consumption of your S/4HANA API license quotas.

  • Mitigation: Implement an adaptive schedule (e.g., poll less frequently overnight/weekends) and routinely monitor API usage logs.

5. Loss of ChgdWhenReleased Granularity

  • The Gap: The standard event model sends precise, field-level delta triggers (e.g., only the schedule time changed). Polling can only see that a change occurred, forcing a full-record refresh to FSM.

  • Impact: FSM automations that depend on specific field triggers (like sending an SMS to a tech only if the priority escalates) cannot be targeted cleanly. FSM will only see a bulk data overwrite.

6. Timestamp-Based Edge Cases

  • The Gap: Delta detection relies strictly on filtering the ServiceDocChangedDateTime field.

  • Impact: Clock drift between CPI and S/4HANA can cause skipped or double-processed records.

    If the CPI local variable storing the Last Run Timestamp is wiped (via redeployment or restart), data will be missed without a manual reset. Boundary records matching the exact timestamp filter might be dropped.

  • Mitigation: Build a 1–2 minute overlap window into your timestamp logic, and set up a monitoring alert for missing or stale timestamp variables.

Conclusion

The absence of SAP Event Mesh is not a roadblock for integrating S/4HANA Cloud service orders with SAP FSM. A custom CPI polling iFlow that mimics an Event Mesh payload offers a viable, non-invasive workaround. By keeping the standard "Replicate Service Order" iFlow completely unmodified, this conservative approach isolates the custom logic into a single, easily maintainable layer.

However, teams must accept the trade-offs: near-real-time latency, the loss of intermediate state history, and added operational complexity around error handling and timestamp management. With a robust retry mechanism and proper monitoring, this architecture can reliably support production workloads.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, S
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #3: Bridging OT and IT: Delivering Mission-Critical Grid Data to SAP BTP Safely, Scalably, and Cleanly

1.      Introduction

Utilities are accelerating their digital transformation, but one architectural frontier remains notoriously difficult to conquer:

How do you safely ingest high-frequency Operational Technology (OT) data from substations, feeders, and Intelligent Electronic Devices (IEDs) into SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) without introducing cyber vulnerabilities or disrupting grid reliability?

Legacy architecture often relied on brittle, custom-coded point-to-point connections or heavy middleware that violated basic security isolation principles. This article provides a comprehensive, production-ready, architecture-driven blueprint for securely integrating:

  • IEC 61850 substation telemetry (GOOSE/MMS)
  • MQTT/Sparkplug B telemetry streams
  • SCADA / RTU / IED asynchronous events
  • Grid-edge IoT sensors

We will achieve this by leverage modern event-driven architectures (EDA), Zero-Trust Network Access (ZTNA), and SAP Clean Core design patterns.

2.      Why OT-to-IT Integration Challenges Enterprise

Bridging the physical grid and the digital cloud requires reconciling two fundamentally opposing engineering philosophies:

Dimension

Operational Technology (OT)

Enterprise Information Technology (IT)

Primary Priority

Availability & Safety: Human life and grid stability. Zero downtime tolerated.

Confidentiality & Integrity: Data security, compliance, and financial accuracy.

Protocols

Industrial, low-overhead, non-IP, or deterministic (IEC 61850, DNP3, Modbus).

Web-native, human-readable, RESTful (HTTPS, OData, gRPC).

Network Philosophy

Air-gapped, strictly segmented (Purdue Model Level 0-3), highly localized.

Cloud-native, perimeterless, distributed, global access.

Data Characteristics

Ultra-high frequency (milliseconds), time-series bursts, massive velocity.

Transactional, structured, stateful, request-response.

To bridge this gap safely, cloud ingestion must be completely outbound-only from the lower levels, strictly audited, cryptographically verified, and decoupled via an enterprise event broker.

3.      Business impact on Utilities

This architecture is not just a technical modernization—it directly enables:

  • Reduced unplanned outages through real-time anomaly detection
  • Lower maintenance costs via predictive asset health insights
  • Improved regulatory compliance with secure audit-ready telemetry flows
  • Faster field response through automated S/4HANA work order generation
  • Scalable grid-edge onboarding without re-platforming legacy OT systems

4.      Reference Architecture: Purdue Model to SAP BTP

The architecture below maps the telemetry flow across the classic Purdue Model for Industrial Control Systems, ensuring that no direct connection is ever established between the cloud and Level 2/3 critical infrastructure.

5.      Architectural Safeguards Highlighted:

  • Directional Isolation: Firewalls block all inbound traffic to the Substation OT network. The Industrial Edge Gateway establishes an outbound connection to the Upper DMZ broker.
  • Decoupling Layer: SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) acts as the shock absorber, ensuring that high-frequency grid telemetry bursts do not overwhelm downstream ERP APIs.

*Architectural Principle: Direct OT-to-cloud integrations without DMZ brokers are inherently unsafe.  Any architecture that bypasses the industrial DMZ violates fundamental Purdue Model isolation and must be avoided.

6.      Deep-Dive: Protocol Translation & Topic Design

 The Conversion: IEC 61850 to Lightweight Cloud Events: IEC 61850 uses Manufacturing Message Specification (MMS) for complex reporting and Generic Object-Oriented Substation Events (GOOSE) for peer-to-peer microsecond trips. These cannot be parsed by cloud apps directly.

An Edge Gateway maps these complex hierarchical Logical Nodes (e.g., MMXU1 for Three-Phase Voltage/Current) into standard JSON structures wrapped in the CloudEvents specification.

Production-Grade Ingestion Payload Example

Below is the exact schema passed from the Upper DMZ to SAP Advanced Event Mesh when a transformer experiences a thermal anomaly:

JSON { "specversion": "1.0",

  "id": "evt-773a-4b92-88ef-22114949ad45", "source": "/us-east/substation-s12/transformer-tx03",   "type":

"com.utility.grid.telemetry.v1",

  "datacontenttype": "application/json",

  "time": "2026-06-01T18:21:55.003Z",

  "data": { "logical_node": "YPTR1",

    "component": "WindingTemperature",

    "metrics": {  "temperature_celsius": 98.4,

      "ambient_celsius": 32.1,

      "cooling_fan_status": "ON_MAX" },

    "status": { "health_score": 72.5,

      "anomaly_detected": true,

      "severity": "WARNING" } } }

Enterprise Topic Hierarchy Best Practices

To optimize routing efficiency within SAP Advanced Event Mesh and avoid bloated subscription wildcards, we enforce a structured, predictable topic hierarchy layout:

$$\text{Domain} \,/\, \text{Business Unit} \,/\, \text{Geographic Zone} \,/\, \text{Substation ID} \,/\, \text{Asset Category} \,/\, \text{Asset ID} \,/\, \text{Event Type}$$

Examples:

  • grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry
  • grid/transmission/midwest/sub-s45/circuit-breaker/cb07/state

This allows BTP consumers to subscribe with surgical precision. For instance, a predictive maintenance engine can listen to all transformer telemetry across the entire Northeast region using the pattern: grid/distribution/northeast/+/transformer/+/telemetry.

7.      The Blueprint in Action: A Practitioner Scenario

To understand how this architecture operates under pressure, let's trace a real-world grid event: A severe thermal anomaly on Substation Transformer TX03 located in the Northeast distribution zone.

Step 1: The Edge Trigger (Purdue Level 2)

  1. The physical temperature sensor on Transformer TX03 detects a sudden spike, hitting $98.4^\circ\text{C}$.
  2. The local Intelligent Electronic Device (IED) broadcasts this telemetry via an IEC 61850 MMS (Manufacturing Message Specification) reporting block over the substation's internal fiber optic bus.

Step 2: Protocol Translation & Hardening (Industrial DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The OT Industrial Edge Gateway (running Litmus Edge) intercepts the raw MMS packet.
  2. It strips out the heavy industrial headers, extracts the data fields from the Logical Node YPTR1 (the standard IEC 61850 node for a power transformer), and maps them into a standardized, web-friendly JSON payload.
  3. The gateway packages this payload inside a standardized CloudEvents v1.0 wrapper.

Step 3: Secure Egress to the DMZ Proxy (Enterprise DMZ Level 3.5)

  1. The Edge Gateway initiates an outbound-only TLS 1.3 connection to the Upper DMZ MQTT Broker (HiveMQ Cluster).
  2. It passes hardware-secured mTLS client certificates to prove its identity and publishes the message to the strict, isolated topic:

 “grid/distribution/northeast/sub-s12/transformer/tx03/telemetry”

Step 4: Ingestion by SAP Advanced Event Mesh (SAP BTP Layer)

  1. The HiveMQ cluster securely routes the incoming stream over a secure WebSocket connection into SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via Dynamic Message Routing (DMR).
  2. Because AEM natively understands the hierarchical topic layout, it instantly clones and routes the message to three concurrent, decoupled subscribers without any database lag:
    • SAP Datasphere Ingestion Queue: For long-term historical time-series storage and grid analytics.
    • SAP AI Core Engine: To recalculate the transformer's Remaining Useful Life (RUL) score.
    • CAP Event Processor Queue: Dedicated to immediate, critical rule evaluation.

Step 5: Real-Time Orchestration & Clean Core Action

  1. A lightweight SAP CAP (Cloud Application Model) microservice consumes the message from its dedicated AEM queue.
  2. The service executes an automated rule: If temperature_celsius > $95^\circ\text{C}$ and cooling_fan_status == "ON_MAX", trigger emergency field intervention.
  3. Rather than writing complex custom ABAP tables inside the ERP, the CAP service uses the standard SAP Integration Suite to call the out-of-the-box S/4HANA OData v4 API (API_EQUIPMENT_SRV).
  4. The Result: An automated Urgent Corrective Maintenance Work Order is instantly generated in SAP S/4HANA Asset Management with a high-priority dispatch flag—all accomplished within 2.3 seconds of the physical temperature spike, leaving the digital core clean, secure, and upgrade-ready.

8.      Advanced Substation-to-BTP Integration Patterns

 *Reality Check*: Batch ingestion architectures are fundamentally incompatible with modern grid telemetry. Millisecond-level data streams require real-time, event-driven processing models—not delayed batch pipelines.

Depending on your edge compute availability and governance requirements, choose one of these production deployment topologies:

Pattern A: Multi-Tiered Message Broker Architecture (Recommended for Zero-Trust)

  • Mechanism: An Edge Gateway pushes local data to an on-premises DMZ MQTT cluster (e.g., Hive MQ). Secure bridge configuration clones and forwards permitted topics out to SAP Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) via cloud connectors or secure WebSocket’s.
  • Pros: Strict isolation; if the cloud connection drops, the local DMZ broker caches events, mitigating data loss.

Pattern B: Intelligent Edge Compute with Stream Processing (Recommended for High Frequency)

  • Mechanism: Deploy an industrial edge compute node (running lightweight runtimes like Node-RED or KubeEdge) physically inside the substation. It aggregates millisecond telemetry, calculates rolling 1-minute means/standard deviations, and publishes only summarized statistical health events to SAP BTP.
  • Pros: Radically reduces egress data costs and avoids overwhelming BTP with trivial data points.

9.      Security & Governance Blueprint (Non-Negotiable Controls)

Bringing OT data to the cloud is impossible without an airtight security architecture. Implement the following parameters at each layer:

  • Mutual TLS (mTLS) with Hardware Roots of Trust: Every edge gateway must authenticate against the DMZ broker using X.509 client certificates issued by an internal Utility Certificate Authority (CA). Private keys must be stored in secure TPM 2.0 hardware chips on the edge device.
  • Granular Access Control Lists (ACLs): Edge gateways must be explicitly barred from publishing to topics outside their assigned asset scope. A compromise of Substation A must never allow injection of malicious events into Substation B.
  • Schema Validation at the Ingress Gate: Use the Schema Validation features in SAP Advanced Event Mesh. If a message arrives containing bad structures, malformed types, or executable code payloads, it is immediately shunted to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for forensics, blocking code injection vectors.
  • Network Partitioning & Backpressure Controls: If the connection between the utility DMZ and SAP BTP degrades, the DMZ broker must utilize Local Storage Buffering. High QoS (Quality of Service) 1 or 2 settings must be applied strictly to critical operational state changes (e.g., breaker trips) to guarantee delivery, while standard continuous metrics use QoS 0 to save bandwidth.

10. Realizing Enterprise Value in SAP BTP

Once normalized grid events stream cleanly into SAP Advanced Event Mesh, they become actionable assets without touching the digital core directly:

*Strategic Insight*: Event Mesh is not optional—it is the control plane for grid-scale ingestion.  Without event-driven decoupling, high-frequency OT telemetry cannot be safely or scalable integrated into enterprise systems.

The Event Consumer Microservices Layer

Deploy lightweight, stateless microservices within the SAP BTP Kyma Runtime or SAP Cloud Application Model (CAP) to process incoming streams:

 

 

 

  • Real-Time Grid Insights: Kyma functions scan the event properties. If a transformer temperature exceeds defined thresholds (temperature_celsius > 95), an immediate event is triggered.
  • AI-Driven Asset Health Optimization: Data streams scale out directly into SAP Datasphere and SAP AI Core. Machine Learning models calculate remaining useful life (RUL) metrics based on historical load/thermal characteristics, sending updated health indices back to operational dashboards.

The Clean Core Imperative

By processing events asynchronously in BTP, the core enterprise system (SAP S/4HANA / IS-U) remains entirely untouched by heavy custom code. S/4HANA only participates when action is required—such as creating an asset maintenance order or executing a field service dispatch via standard standard OData v4 APIs. The system updates and upgrades effortlessly, unencumbered by massive custom databases full of RAW time-series data.

11. End-to-End Flow: Transformer Overheating Scenario

  • A transformer in Substation S12 exceeds thermal threshold (98.4°C).
  • IEC 61850 MMS reports are captured by Edge Gateway.
  • Gateway converts payload to Cloud Events JSON.
  • Event is securely published (mTLS) to DMZ MQTT broker.
  • Broker forwards allowed topics to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.
  • AEM routes the event to a CAP microservice.
  • CAP evaluates rules → triggers anomaly event.
  • SAP AI Core enriches with predictive health score.
  • SAP S/4HANA automatically creates a maintenance work order.
  • Field service team is dispatched proactively.

12. Operational Summary Blueprint

  1. The convergence of OT and IT is no longer optional—it is foundational to modern utilities.
  2. However, success depends not on connectivity alone, but on *how* that connectivity is governed. 
  3. Architectures that prioritize strict isolation, event-driven decoupling, and Clean Core principles will define the next generation of grid intelligence.
  4. SAP BTP, when used correctly, becomes not just an integration platform—but the control tower for real-time, autonomous utility operations.

entry.title: Connection Monitoring: Real-Time Connection Visibility for Event Mesh in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

When you run a messaging service like SAP Event Mesh in SAP Integration Suite, you quickly accumulate a wide mix of messaging clients and their connections — AMQP-based integrations, MQTT devices, REST publishers, and webhooks. Until now, understanding who is connected, how many connections are active, and which protocols they use meant digging into logs or asking the platform team.

Connection Monitoring changes that. It is now generally available in the Event Mesh in the SAP Integration Suite monitoring UI, as a dedicated screen that provides a real-time, protocol-aware view of all active messaging connections on your service instance.

What is Connection Monitoring in Event Mesh in SAP Integration Suite?

Connection Monitoring provides a live, protocol-aware view of all active messaging connections on a service instance, directly in the Event Mesh in the SAP Integration Suite monitoring UI. You can see which message clients are connected, which protocols they are using, and how that usage has evolved over time.

Key Capabilities

  • Real-time connection overview: A time-series line chart and client table give you an immediate snapshot of all active connections across every message client on the service instance.
  • Protocol-level breakdown: Connections are categorized by protocol — AMQP, MQTT, Webhook, and REST — so you can understand not just whether a client is connected, but how it is connected.
  • Per-client drill-down: Selecting any message client opens a Detail view with a dedicated time-series line chart showing connection details for that client alone, making it easy to isolate anomalies.
  • Chart personalization: Following the SAP Fiori Analytical List Page pattern, the overview provides some standard chart personalization controls — time range selection, zoom, legend selection, and fullscreen mode — letting you tailor the visualization to the window of interest without leaving the screen.
